title: Market minds: Why Australia is the world's most underappreciated AI trade
full_text:
The dominant market narrative is simple: if investors want exposure to artificial intelligence, they need to go offshore. Buy the chipmakers. Buy the hyperscalers. Buy the magnificent seven. It is a neat story, but it is narrow and incorrect.
AI is not only a software or semiconductor story. It is also an infrastructure build-out. Every model, every training run and every query ultimately depends on physical assets - data centres, power grids, cooling systems, fibre, switchboards, transformers and raw materials.
The economic value does not sit only with the companies designing the models. It also accrues to the companies providing the backbone that allows those models to operate at scale.
This is where Australia is far more relevant than many investors appreciate. Our market - with banks, miners, supermarkets and property - is often dismissed as too "old economy" to offer meaningful AI exposure. I would argue the opposite.
The International Energy Agency expects global data centre electricity consumption to more than double from around 415 terawatt hours in 2024 to about 945 terawatt hours by 2030, growing at roughly 15 per cent a year over that period.
In Australia, deployable data centre capacity is forecast to more than double from 1350 megawatts in 2024 to 3100 megawatts by 2030, attracting more than $26 billion of additional investment. These numbers, even if optimistic, are extraordinary and change the investment question. The constraint is no longer only algorithms or chips - it is power, land, grid connection, cooling, construction and speed of delivery.
This is why the AI trade should not be viewed purely through the lens of Nvidia, Microsoft and other US technology leaders. The winners will also include the companies that build, wire, power and supply the infrastructure.
Australia has quietly become a key node in the global AI infrastructure build-out.
It has political stability, proximity to Asia, deep pools of institutional capital and growing demand for sovereign compute. The major global tech groups are already investing here, alongside local operators and developers.
This is the part of the market investors are underestimating. Australia is not trying to compete with Silicon Valley on foundation models or GPU design. Our opportunity is different.
It is to provide the land, power, connectivity, construction capability and materials that make AI infrastructure possible - not just domestically, but often internationally.
In Australia, the first-round beneficiaries of the AI thematic are the infrastructure owners and developers. NextDC, Macquarie Technology and DigiCo Infrastructure REIT sit close to the centre of the build-out through data centre's and compute environments.
NextDC has signed a memorandum of understanding with OpenAI around sovereign AI infrastructure at its S7 hyperscale campus at Eastern Creek. Macquarie Technology's IC3 Super West is being purpose-built for AI and cloud workloads, with 47 megawatts of capacity and secured end-state power.
Goodman Group is another important exposure. It has increasingly become a data centre infrastructure developer, as well as a logistics property owner. Its global data centre power bank now spans 6.4 gigawatts across completed facilities, secured power and potential projects.
Then there is connectivity. Megaport provides the cloud interconnection and network plumbing that supports data movement between enterprises, data centres and cloud platforms.
We think the second-round beneficiaries may be even more interesting. These are the picks-and-shovels companies that the market still tends to overlook, such as electrical contractors, switchboard manufacturers, power distributors and infrastructure suppliers.
Southern Cross Electrical Engineering and GenusPlus are examples of businesses exposed to the physical build-out through high-voltage cabling, switchboards, transformers, UPS systems, power distribution, security, communications and structured cabling.
These are not speculative technology names. These are companies with order books, physical assets, contracts and tangible cash flows.
Perhaps the most underappreciated exposure for Australian investors sits in resources, particularly copper. AI data centres are extraordinarily power-intensive.
Copper is embedded across the system: grid connection, transmission, transformers, switchboards, internal cabling, cooling infrastructure and backup power.
The more power-hungry AI becomes, the more copper-intensive the infrastructure becomes.
BHP estimates copper used in data centres globally could grow six-fold by 2050, from around half a million tonnes a year today to about three million tonnes a year.
S&P Global forecasts data centre copper demand rising from 1.1 million tonnes in 2025 to 2.5 million tonnes by 2040, with AI training data centres accounting for the majority of data centre copper demand by 2030.
Australia may not make the GPUs, but we are a major supplier of the inputs that make GPUs useful at scale. The marginal dollar of AI investment increasingly flows not only into software margins, but into physical infrastructure: cables, transformers, powered land, cooling and energy.
That is precisely where Australia has leverage.
We think investors are making the mistake of defining AI too narrowly and only looking offshore. In every major infrastructure cycle - railroads, electrification, the internet and cloud computing - the most obvious winners were not always the only winners.
Often, the more durable returns came from the companies providing the enabling infrastructure.
Jun Bei Liu is the founder and lead portfolio manager at Ten Cap.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Jun Bei Liu
heading: Data Science and AI
